elements.sent-2411-001: We consider the Courant-sharp eigenvalues of the Robin Laplacian for bounded, connected, open sets in $\R^n$, $n \geq 2$, with Lipschitz boundary.
elements.sent-2411-002: We prove Pleijel's theorem which implies that there are only finitely many Courant-sharp eigenvalues in this setting as well as an improved version of Pleijel's theorem, extending previously known results that required more regularity of the boundary.
elements.sent-2411-003: In addition, we obtain an upper bound for the number of Courant-sharp Robin eigenvalues of a bounded, connected, convex, open set in $\R^n$ with $C^2$ boundary that is explicit in terms of the geometric quantities of the set and the norm sup of the negative part of the Robin parameter.
elements.sent-2411-004: Let $\Omega \subset \R^n$ be a bounded, connected, open set with Lipschitz boundary.
elements.sent-2411-006: We consider the eigenvalue problem for the Robin Laplacian \begin{equation}\label{eq:roblap} \begin{cases} \Delta u = \mu u \text{ in } \Omega, \partial_\nu u + h u = 0 \text{ on } \partial \Omega, \end{cases}\end{equation} where $\Delta = - \dive \grad$ is the positive Laplacian and $\nu$ is the unit outward-pointing normal along $\partial \Omega$.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{eq:roblap}  \Delta u = \mu u  in  \Omega, \partial_\nu u + h u = 0  on  \partial \Omega, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-007: The eigenvalues of the Robin Laplacian on $L^2(\Omega)$, counted with multiplicity, can be written as \begin{equation}\mu_1(\Omega, h) \leq \mu_2(\Omega, h) \leq \dots \leq \mu_k(\Omega, h) \leq \dots \nearrow +\infty.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-008: We use the notation $\Delta_\Omega^{R,h}$ to refer to the Robin Laplacian in \eqref{eq:roblap .}
elements.sent-2411-009: The case where $h \equiv 0$ corresponds to the Neumann Laplacian and the case where $h \to +\infty$ corresponds to the Dirichlet Laplacian.
elements.sent-2411-010: Let $u_k$ be an eigenfunction of $\Delta_\Omega^{R,h}$ corresponding to the $k$-th eigenvalue $\mu_k(\Omega, h)$.
elements.sent-2411-011: The connected components of $\Omega \setminus \overline{\{x \in \Omega : u_k(x) = 0\}}$ are called nodal domains of $u_k$.
elements.sent-2411-012: Courant's Nodal Domain theorem asserts that any eigenfunction $u_k$ corresponding to the $k$-th eigenvalue $\mu_k(\Omega, h)$ has at most $k$ nodal domains.
elements.sent-2411-013: In the case where $\mu_k(\Omega, h)$ has an eigenfunction with exactly $k$ nodal domains, we say that $\mu_k(\Omega, h)$ is a Courant-sharp eigenvalue and $u_k$ is a Courant-sharp eigenfunction.
elements.sent-2411-014: Let $\mathcal{N _\Omega^h(k)$ denote the number of nodal domains of the eigenfunction of $\Delta_\Omega^{R,h}$ corresponding to the $k$-th eigenvalue (counted with multiplicities).}
elements.sent-2411-015: Pleijel's theorem \cite{Pl56,BM82 asserts that for a bounded, connected, open set $\Omega \subset \R^n$, \begin{equation}\label{eq:pldir} \limsup_{k\to\infty}\frac{\nodal_\Omega^{+\infty}(k)}{k}\le \gamma(n),\end{equation} where $\gamma(n):=(2\pi)^n/\omega_n^2 j^n_{\frac{n-2 {2 <1,$ $\omega_n$ is the Lebesgue measure of a ball in $\R^n$ of radius $1$ and $j_{\frac{n-1 {2 }$ is the smallest positive zero of the Bessel function $J_{\frac{n-2 {2 }$.}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-019: Pleijel's theorem implies that there are only finitely many Courant-sharp Dirichlet eigenvalues of $\Omega$.
elements.sent-2411-020: This result, called the weak Pleijel theorem, holds for any open set $\Omega \subset \R^n$ of finite Lebesgue measure (see, e.g., \cite{vdBkG16cs ).}
elements.sent-2411-021: The extension of Pleijel's theorem to the Neumann and Robin eigenvalue problems, as well as investigating geometric bounds on Courant-sharp eigenvalues and the number of them, has been the subject of several studies summarised below over the last few years.

elements.section-2411-005: Geometric upper bounds for the number of Courant-sharp Robin eigenvalues
elements.sent-2411-226: The goal of this section is to obtain an upper bound for the number of Courant-sharp Robin eigenvalues of an open, bounded, connected, convex set $\Omega \subset \R^n$, $ n \geq 2$, with $C^2$ boundary, that is explicit in terms of the geometric quantities of $\Omega$ and the Robin parameter.
elements.sent-2411-227: To do this, we first derive a comparison between the Robin and Neumann counting functions for open, bounded, connected, Lipschitz sets in $\R^n$.
elements.sent-2411-228: We then derive an upper bound for the largest positive Courant-sharp Robin eigenvalue of an open, bounded, connected set $\Omega \subset \R^n$ with $C^2$ boundary.
elements.sent-2411-229: By combining these two results with the result from \cite[Appendix A]{GL18 which holds for convex sets, we obtain an upper bound for the number of Courant-sharp Robin eigenvalues of $\Omega$.}
elements.sent-2411-230: We consider the case where $\Omega$ has $C^2$ boundary as this setting allows us to glean explicit geometric control in the geometric bounds that follow.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Comparison between Neumann and Robin counting functions
elements.sent-2411-231: In order to use previous work estimating the Neumann eigenvalues and the Neumann counting function, we prove, for an arbitrary Lipschitz domain, a comparison result between the Neumann and the Robin spectra.
elements.sent-2411-232: This can be stated either as a lower bound for the Robin eigenvalues or an upper bound for the Robin counting function.
elements.sent-2411-233: For $\mu > 0$, we define the Robin counting function as \begin{equation}N_\Omega^h(\mu) :=\sharp\{k\in\N\,:\,\mu_k(\Omega,h)<\mu\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-234: We denote the Neumann counting function by $N_\Omega^N(\mu) = N_\Omega^0(\mu)$.
elements.sent-2411-235: We recall that for any Lipschitz domain $\Omega$ with outward-pointing vector field $F$ and for all $u\in H^1(\Omega)$, we have \begin{equation}\label{eqTrace} \int_{\partial \Omega}u^2\, ds \le \Gamma_1(\Omega,F)\,\int_{\Omega}u^2\,dx+\Gamma_2(\Omega,F)\left(\int_{\Omega}u^2\,dx\right)^{\frac12}\left(\int_{\Omega}\left|\nabla u\right|^2\,dx\right)^{\frac12},\end{equation} with $\Gamma_1(\Omega,F)$ and $\Gamma_2(\Omega,F)$ the constants defined in Proposition \ref{prop:keyinequality .}
